author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay summarizes a UCL paper (arXiv:2604.14228) dissecting Claude Code's architecture: only 1.6% of the codebase is AI decision logic while 98.4% is operational harness (permission gates with 7 modes, tool routing, a 5-layer context compaction pipeline, subagents that return only summaries). Core thesis: as frontier models converge on raw coding ability, harness quality, not the model, becomes the differentiator.
content: Claude Code fully dissected. UCL researchers reverse-engineered the leaked Claude source. Only 1.6% of the codebase is AI decision logic; the other 98.4% is operational infrastructure: permission gates, tool routing, context compaction, recovery, session persistence. The core loop is a simple while-true. A permission system with 7 modes and an ML classifier; a 5-layer context compaction pipeline; four extension mechanisms ordered by context cost (hooks, skills, plugins, MCP); subagents return only summary text. The bet: as models converge on coding ability, harness quality is the differentiator. Paper: Dive into Claude Code (arXiv:2604.14228).

author: Alex Ker
subject: Post by Alex Ker 🔭 on X
summary: Alex Ker's deep-dive guide on optimizing AI coding harnesses: keep config/.md files lean and human-written (frontier LLMs hit a "dumb zone" past a few hundred instructions), use progressive disclosure for CLIs/skills/MCP tools, structure prompts with HumanLayer's Research-Plan-Implement (R.P.I.) framework, and use subagents (parallel fan-out for breadth, pipelines for depth) to keep the main context clean. Core argument: the harness, not the model, is where engineering judgment compounds, so commit to one and iterate.
content: Harnesses are everything. Three things to optimize: keep .md/config files lean and human-written (instruction budget; LLM-generated system prompts degrade performance and cost ~20% more), apply progressive disclosure across CLIs/skills/MCP tools (Claude Code's MCP tool search cuts context ~85%), structure prompts with HumanLayer's R.P.I. (Research, Plan, Implement) framework, and use subagents via parallel fan-out and pipelines to keep the main context clean. The best harness is the one you customize and iterate on; commit instead of constantly switching.

author: Eric Hartford
subject: Post by Eric Hartford on X
summary: Eric Hartford releases Clearwing, an MIT-licensed open-source vulnerability-discovery engine that reproduces Anthropic's "Project Glasswing" results with any LLM. His argument: the real innovation isn't the gated Claude Mythos model but the model-agnostic pipeline, rank files by attack surface, fan out hundreds of file-scoped agents, use crash oracles (AddressSanitizer/UBSan) as ground truth, and run a verification agent to filter noise. Reproduced findings with OpenAI Codex 5.4 and a Qwen3.5 finetune.
content:
Anthropic announced Project Glasswing alongside Claude Mythos Preview, finding thousands of zero-days. But the real innovation isn't the model, it's the workflow: rank every file by attack surface, fan out hundreds of parallel agents each scoped to one file, use crash oracles (AddressSanitizer, UBSan) as ground truth, run a second verification agent to filter noise, generate exploits as a triage mechanism. Pipelines are model-agnostic. Clearwing is a fully open-source vulnerability discovery engine that works with any LLM; reproduced Glasswing's findings with OpenAI Codex 5.4. MIT licensed.

GitHub - Lazarus-AI/clearwing

content:
Hermes Agent from Nous Research, a new standard of AI-trading on Polymarket. It is an open self-hosted agent that self-learns (writes itself a skill after every complex task), has persistent memory (MEMORY.md + USER.md + SQLite), and works 24/7 on a VPS with Telegram alerts. Closed learning loop: every ~15 tool calls it analyzes what worked and improves itself. Install Hermes, copy 7 prompts, launch on VPS.

Hermes Agent + Polymarket: how I built a self-learning weather trading bot. A month ago @NousResearch released Hermes.

author: Hamudi
subject: Post by Hamudi on X
summary: Hamudi ties into Sequoia's "$1T thesis" that AI sells outcomes (work) rather than software tools, introducing "Outcome Primitives" as a way to measure economic outcomes, citing a published paper tracking 1,300 agents over 21 days that created $32M in value (jobs secured, $200k grants won, e-commerce shops shipped). Framing: copilots compete on software margins and lose to big labs; outcome engines compete on services margins.
content:
Silicon Valley is waking up to the $1T thesis by Sequoia. SaaS sold tools; AI sells outcomes. But you can't build an outcome engine if you don't know how to measure an outcome. We published a paper on measuring economic outcomes, which we call Outcome Primitives. We tracked 1,300 agents over 21 days creating $32M of economic value: securing jobs, winning $200k grants, shipping e-commerce shops. If your AI product is just a copilot, you compete on software margins; if it delivers the primitive, you compete on services margins.

Sequoia's thesis that the next $1T company will sell work, not software, is the most important reframe in AI right now. Sell a copilot and you compete with every model release; sell the outcome and you transform industries.

content: Resolvers: The Routing Table for Intelligence. A resolver is a routing table for context. When task type X appears, load document Y first. That's it. One sentence. But that one sentence is the difference between an agent that compounds intelligence vs one that starts from scratch every time. Built on the Thin Harness, Fat Skills framework introduced previously.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay argues agent memory is three-dimensional, needing a relational store for provenance, a vector store for semantics, and a graph store for relationships, because flat vector search misses multi-hop connections (the "bridge" fact that links two entities). He points to Cognee, an open-source project that unifies all three behind four async calls (default embedded SQLite+LanceDB+Kuzu, swappable for Postgres/Qdrant/Neo4j).
content:
Agent memory is three-dimensional. Most systems use a single vector store, capturing only one dimension. Vector search misses bridge facts that connect entities across two hops. The three dimensions: a relational store for provenance (source, time, access), a vector store for semantics, and a graph store for relationships. Enter through vectors, traverse the graph, ground every result in provenance. Cognee is an open-source project unifying all three behind four async calls; default embedded stack is SQLite + LanceDB + Kuzu, swap in Postgres/Qdrant/Neo4j for production.

Build Agents that never forget: a first-principles walk through agent memory, from Python lists to markdown files to vector search to graph-vector hybrids.

Cognee on GitHub

Each subagent researches, writes a critical assessment, then implements high-confidence fixes.
content: To clean up vibecoded slop, use this prompt with 8 subagents, one each for: (1) deduplicate and apply DRY, (2) consolidate shared type definitions, (3) find and remove unused code with knip, (4) untangle circular dependencies with madge, (5) remove weak types like any/unknown with researched strong types, (6) remove defensive try/catch that hides errors, (7) remove deprecated/legacy/fallback code, (8) remove AI slop, stubs and unhelpful comments. Each subagent does detailed research, writes a critical assessment with recommendations, then implements all high-confidence recommendations.

subject: Post by klöss on X
summary: klss argues agents fail across repos because of unstructured markdown, and proposes a four-folder convention to remove ambiguity: /audits (proof), /docs (description), /plans (intent), /specs (law). Separating intent from proof from law stops Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor agents from hallucinating context.
content:
Your agents fail because your repo has no structure. Four folders for zero ambiguity: /audits (proof/verify), /docs (description/describe), /plans (intent/plan), /specs (law/enforce). Separate intent from proof from law from descriptions and agents stop guessing what files mean.

content: Guide to running BIG B0Is on your small hardware. 1. Use REAPs: up to 50% savings. 2. Use quantisations: 75% savings - AWQ / GPTQ / W4A16 / FP8 = FAST inference - GGUF / EXL3 = Slow but just works - MLX = Best for apple. 3. Use 8bit KV cache: 50-75% savings.

author: Alpha Batcher
subject: Post by Alpha Batcher on X
summary: Distills architectural principles for production AI agents by quoting Rohit's article reverse-engineering Claude Code's architecture (github.com/rohit4verse). Key patterns: async generators for streaming, parallel read-only tools vs serial write tools, tools executing during generation (not after), system prompt designed for cache efficiency, hierarchical context compaction cheapest-first, isolated worktree per sub-agent. Calls it the most detailed public breakdown of a production agent architecture available.
content: Architectural principles for building your own AI agent: async generator instead of while loop (streaming native), tools classified as read-only run parallel/write tools run serial, tools execute during generation not after, system prompt designed for cache efficiency, hierarchical compaction cheapest first, every error has recovery path inside the loop, each sub-agent gets isolated worktree and context. Infrastructure is the fourth layer without it you have a demo not a product.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Comparison of how Anthropic, OpenAI, CrewAI, and LangChain approach the agent harness differently. The one agreement: the model is not the product, the infrastructure around it is. Anthropic bets on a deliberately thin "dumb loop" harness where the model makes all decisions. OpenAI takes a similar but slightly thicker approach. CrewAI and LangChain bet on heavier orchestration infrastructure. The core architectural question: as models get smarter, do you need less infrastructure or more?
content: What does every big company think about the agent harness? Anthropic, OpenAI, CrewAI, LangChain. They all build agents. They all wrap their models in infrastructure to make them useful. They each call it the harness. But they agree on one thing. And disagree on everything else. The agreement: the model is not the product. The infrastructure around the model is. The disagreement: how much of that infrastructure should exist.

author: Sigrid Jin
subject: Post by Sigrid Jin 🌈🙏 on X
summary: Summary of "Better Harness" paper on using evals as a flywheel for agent improvement. Key insight: evals are the new training data — instead of updating weights, you update the agent harness. Warns that agents reward-hack evals, so you need strict train/test splits. Quality over quantity in eval design. The flywheel: mine prod traces for failures, turn into evals, auto-tweak prompts/tools, validate, repeat.
content: Evals are the new training data. Instead of updating weights, you are updating the agent harness. Problem is agents are famous cheaters. They will reward-hack your evals and overfit just to make the score go up. Solution is treat evals like real ML. You need strict train/test splits and holdout sets to force actual generalization. Quality over quantity.

author: Aakash Gupta
subject: Post by Aakash Gupta on X
summary: The "advisor pattern" for AI agent cost optimization: run Sonnet for routine execution ($3/$15 per MTok) and fire a tool call to Opus only for genuine decision points. Both models share full context, eliminating the fragmentation problem in multi-model architectures. Claude Code has been doing this internally. Practical architecture for any company hitting the cost wall with frontier models in production agent loops.
content: This diagram is the entire AI agent cost problem solved in one architecture. Every company building agents in production hits the same wall. You need frontier-model reasoning for the hard decisions, but 90% of agent turns are routine execution: reading files, calling APIs, writing boilerplate. Paying Opus prices for those turns is like hiring a surgeon to take your blood pressure. The advisor pattern splits the loop. Sonnet runs every turn at $3/$15 per MTok. When the agent hits a genuine decision point, it fires a tool call to Opus. Opus reads the full shared context, sends back strategic advice, and Sonnet continues executing. The shared context is what makes this work.

author: Ashpreet Bedi
subject: Post by Ashpreet Bedi on X
summary: Ashpreet Bedi argues that building agentic software requires systems engineering discipline — you can't optimize AI agent systems by optimizing individual components. Draws parallels to Bell Labs' 1940s discovery that telephone network behavior emerged from component interactions, not individual parts. The current wave of 'harness engineering' is repeating 80-year-old mistakes.
content: Systems Engineering: Building Agentic Software That Works. In the early 1940s, Bell Labs was building the national telephone network and discovered you can't optimize a system by optimizing individual components. The agentic software ecosystem is repeating the same mistakes that spawned systems engineering. Current harness engineering asks you to use filesystem for storage and memory, then tries to work around limitations. The whole-system perspective matters — interactions between components determine behavior, not the components themselves.

author: Allie K. Miller
subject: Post by Allie K. Miller on X
summary: Allie K. Miller shares a 5-level 'Proactive AI-First Team Member' framework for hiring and onboarding. Levels range from 1 (not using AI) to 5 (full ownership with AI-augmented critical thinking). Key insight: most candidates interview at level 3 (solution-oriented but not action-oriented) — she wants new hires starting at level 4 (action-oriented with technical/business tradeoff awareness). Originally from @businessbarista and @stephsmithio, with AI additions by Miller.
content:
I stole this framework, and it has genuinely been one of the most helpful hiring and onboarding guides my team uses.

Being AI-first means nothing without solving real business problems. It's not enough to just use AI. I know plenty of folks using AI dozens of times a day, 7 days a week, who are still working at a Level 2. Even worse, they have no idea they're stuck as a surface user.

You have to use AI for faster experimentation. To shorten the iteration cycle. To improve the actual outcome. Without those three, you're just wasting tokens.

Most people I interview are at a 3 (solution-oriented, but not action-oriented). I want everyone to start at a 4 (action-oriented with a sense of technical, user, and business tradeoffs). And when they earn trust, we move up to a level 5 (full ownership of the problem, solution, and continued management of the work).

Using AI doesn't replace your critical thinking. It means the work you can pull off now wasn't on the table a year ago, and your job is getting bigger.

Save this for your next new hire.

Source: this was a framework first introduced to me by Alex (@businessbarista) who was introduced to it by Steph (@stephsmithio). I added the AI parts.

[Includes "The Proactive AI-First Team Member" pyramid framework image with 5 levels]

content: Karpathy's new set-up is the ultimate self-improving second brain, and it takes zero manual editing. It acts as a living AI knowledge base that actually heals itself. Instead of relying on complex RAG, the LLM pulls raw research directly into an Obsidian Markdown wiki. It completely takes over: Index creation, System linting, Native Q&A routing. The core process: You dump raw sources into a folder, the LLM auto-compiles an indexed .md wiki, you ask complex questions, it generates outputs (Marp slides, matplotlib plots) and files them back in. When agents maintain their own memory layer, they don't need massive, expensive context limits. They just need clean file organization and the ability to query their own indexes.

author: Rohit
subject: Post by Rohit on X
summary: Rohit argues top AI teams win not on model selection but on 'harness engineering' — how you design the agent's interface, manage context windows, cap search results, run linters at edit time, and maintain persistent state files. A teaser thread for an 8,000-word deep dive covering 8 actionable principles for building more reliable AI agents; key insights include: interface shapes model reasoning, context pollution is costly, and forced query refinement beats flooding with results.
content:
The best AI teams are not winning on models. They are winning on harness engineering. And almost nobody is talking about it.

I spent a week writing 8,000 words on harness engineering.

Here's what actually matters, in 8 points:

>The interface is not decoration. For an LLM agent, the interface is the mind.
>The context window is not a RAM slot. Every irrelevant token you dump into it competes with the information that actually matters.
>Capping search results outperforms returning everything. When you force the agent to refine its query instead of flooding it with matches, you turn a failure mode into a feedback loop.
>Run a linter at edit time, not at test time. A syntax error caught at the moment of writing costs nothing. The same error caught ten steps later cascades into a debugging spiral that burns your entire context budget.
>A persistent progress file is the cheapest fix in agentic systems.

subject: Post by Greg Pstrucha on X
summary: Greg Pstrucha demonstrates how malicious Claude Code skills can hide instructions inside PNGs and abuse Claude Code's 'expand output' feature to fool both humans and agents — a real security threat. content: skills are a real attack vector, i've been playing with ways in which malicious skills can hide instructions to fool humans and agents. I tested some known scanners and they haven't caught quite a few of these issues. you should only pull from trusted sources. I've improved skill-scanner (github.com/getsentry/skills) - you can run it directly in claude code or run Warden (warden.sentry.dev). Favorites: Hiding instructions inside PNG; Abusing Claude Code's expand output into skills functionality.

author: elvis
subject: Post by elvis on X
summary: elvis highlights EvoSkill, a self-evolving multi-agent framework that automatically discovers and refines agent skills through iterative failure analysis. Three agents (Executor, Proposer, Skill-Builder) drive the loop, with a Pareto frontier retaining only skills that improve held-out validation while the base model stays frozen. Reported gains: Claude Code w/ Opus 4.5 from 60.6%->67.9% on OfficeQA, +12.1% on SealQA, and +5.3% zero-shot transfer to BrowseComp.
notes: Aligns with wiki topic: Skills as graphs. Self-evolving skills concept.
content:
A self-evolving framework to discover and refine agent skills. Most agent skills today are hand-crafted or poorly designed by an agent. Multi-agent systems for building skills look promising. EvoSkill is a self-evolving framework that automatically discovers and refines agent skills through iterative failure analysis: it analyzes execution failures, proposes new skills or edits, and materializes them into structured reusable skill folders. Three collaborating agents drive the process: an Executor that runs tasks, a Proposer that diagnoses failures, and a Skill-Builder that creates concrete skill folders. A Pareto frontier governs selection, retaining only skills that improve held-out validation performance while keeping the model frozen. On OfficeQA, EvoSkill improves Claude Code with Opus 4.5 from 60.6% to 67.9% exact-match accuracy; on SealQA it yields a 12.1% gain; skills evolved on SealQA transfer zero-shot to BrowseComp, improving accuracy by 5.3% without modification.

Paper: EvoSkill (arxiv.org/abs/2603.02766)

author: Anish Moonka
subject: Post by Anish Moonka on X
summary: Anish Moonka's 12-point notes from Boris Cherny (Head of Claude Code) on Lenny's Podcast: coding is largely solved (Boris ships 10-30 PRs/day, no hand-written code since Nov 2025); the next frontier is AI deciding what to build; productivity per Anthropic engineer is up 200%; underfund teams on purpose; give engineers unlimited tokens; the Bitter Lesson favors general models over rigid orchestration; build for the model six months out; latent demand is the best product signal; 'software engineer' is becoming 'builder'; mechanistic interpretability enables layered safety; and 70% of engineers/PMs enjoy their jobs more now.
notes: Essential reading. Covers strategy, team structure, and where software is heading.
content:
Notes from Boris Cherny (Head of Claude Code, Anthropic) on Lenny's Podcast on what happens after coding is solved. 1) Coding is largely solved — Boris hasn't hand-edited code since Nov 2025 and ships 10-30 PRs/day via Claude Code. 2) The next frontier is AI deciding what to build (scanning Slack feedback, bug reports, telemetry, proposing its own PRs). 3) Productivity per engineer at Anthropic is up 200%. 4) Underfund your teams on purpose — one engineer with unlimited tokens ships faster; Cowork was built by a small team in 10 days. 5) Give engineers unlimited tokens as the new hiring perk. 6) The Bitter Lesson applies to everything — rigid orchestration gives 10-20% gains that get wiped by the next model; give the model tools and a goal. 7) Build for the model six months from now. 8) Latent demand is the best product signal (Marketplace, Cowork). 9) 'Software engineer' is going away, replaced by 'builder'; the strongest people are generalists. 10) The printing press is the right analogy — we are the scribes, freed from tedious copying. 11) Anthropic can peek inside the model's brain via mechanistic interpretability; three layers of safety. 12) 70% of engineers and PMs enjoy their jobs more now (designers less so).

Boris Cherny ~90 min on Lenny's Podcast about what happens after coding is solved.

content:
I think this must be the most well researched technical article on X. I've learnt more about SQL databases in this article than my whole career. The argument of plausible vs correctness in LLM code outputs is so well articulated. Highly recommended read.

Article: Your LLM Doesn't Write Correct Code. It Writes Plausible Code. A simple test: a primary key lookup on 100 rows. SQLite takes 0.09 ms; an LLM-generated Rust rewrite takes 1,815.43 ms. It's not a misplaced comma.

author: staysaasy
subject: Post by staysaasy on X
summary: staysaasy long-form article 'Avoiding a Culture of Emergencies': the best managers have far fewer preventable emergencies because they know how hard things actually are, know what's important and can say no, hold a strong mental model of their team and company to forecast needs, and genuinely care. Emergencies are largely a management choice, and avoiding them is one of the best talent-retention mechanisms.
notes: Not AI-specific but relevant to team health as we navigate rapid AI adoption.
content: Avoiding a Culture of Emergencies. There's enormous variability in how often teams have emergencies, and better managers have far fewer preventable ones. Four copyable factors: (1) Good managers know how hard things are — they're deep experts, ask clarifying questions, and set expectations. (2) Good managers know what's important and have the conviction to push back on artificial emergencies. (3) Good managers have a strong mental model of their team and company so they can forecast future needs (e.g., investing early in AI prototyping). (4) Good managers care about their team's long-term well-being over short-term career boosts. Emergencies are often a choice; avoiding them is one of the best talent-retention mechanisms that exists.

author: Dan Robinson

summary: Argues issue tracking/backlogs are dying: forward-thinking AI-augmented teams skip Linear/tickets entirely because the cost to fix an issue now approaches the cost to understand it. content: Dan Robinson (@danlovesproofs) — "The Death of Issue Tracking". Notices forward-thinking engineering teams running with no Linear, no backlog, no issue tracking at all, scaling this to 30+ engineers. Post-backlog thinking: if it matters it'll come up again; why write a ticket when you can just fix it (cost to fix is converging on cost to understand); a blank page forces a real OODA loop and better prioritization. Tailwind is AI — 7 capable engineers running agent swarms deliver what took 50+. Requires small flat teams, high ambient context, engineers trusted to make independent judgment calls and do their own PM, and agent-ready dev infra. author: David Byttow
subject: Post by David Byttow on X
summary: Essay arguing AI agents are collapsing the coordination layer of organizations. As execution and coordination costs approach zero, the remaining scarce skill is goal clarity, taste, and ownership. Warns bad judgment now scales faster — strategic mistakes show up as fast delivery of the wrong thing.
notes: X article — strong think piece on AI eliminating coordination overhead in engineering orgs
content: David Byttow (@davidbyttow) — "AI absorbed the process layer". Agents are collapsing the coordination layer (task decomposition, sprint planning, dependency graphs, status syncs). Writing code was never the bottleneck — coordination overhead was, and that's commoditizing. What remains is judgment, taste, direction, ownership, and the ability to define a goal precisely enough that autonomous systems can act on it. AI won't replace managers; it exposes the ones whose contribution was procedural rather than directional. Leverage inverts: one sharp operator orchestrating agent fleets covers what took 5–10 people. Darker side: bad judgment scales faster — strip away the natural checkpoints of the coordination layer and strategic mistakes announce themselves through fast delivery of the wrong thing. Selection pressure on leadership judgment is about to intensify.

author: tetsuo
subject: Post by tetsuo on X
summary: Technical breakdown of recurring agentic failure modes in fast/distilled code models: wrong direct-exec vs shell selection, structured-output (JSON-only) non-compliance, and tool-result grounding failures (reporting success after a tool error). Fix: distill full agent trajectories (request→tool→output→grounded response), add contrastive near-miss examples, and gate on concrete agentic evals.
notes: X article — deep technical analysis of where distilled models fail at agentic tasks
content: tetsuo (@tetsuoai) — "Agentic Fine-Tuning Gaps in Fast and Distilled Models". Three recurring failure modes that base models get right but distilled/fast models miss: (1) Action selection & API semantics — direct execution (command+args) vs shell invocation; needs contrastive near-miss examples (wrong call, correct call, why). (2) Instruction priority & output discipline — 'return JSON only' compliance; training teaches the habit, constrained decoding enforces the contract. (3) State tracking & belief updating — the worst one: model gets an explicit tool error then reports success anyway; it's pattern-completing, not grounding. Fix: distill full agent trajectories (user request → tool choice → tool output → grounded response), not just final answers. Eval gates: argv correctness, JSON validity/schema exactness, contradiction rate. Includes a detailed eval prompt covering all three modes, and a note that desktop tools in Docker can't reach the host's localhost.

author: Sandhya
subject: Post by Sandhya on X
summary: 'BREAKING'-style but substantive thread on LMCache, an open-source KV-cache layer (6.9K stars, used by Google Cloud, CoreWeave, NVIDIA) that makes the LLM KV cache persistent and shareable across engine instances, tiered GPU→DRAM→disk→S3. Enables instant RAG, disaggregated prefill, and context sharing; integrates with vLLM and SGLang. Apache-2.0, pip install lmcache.
notes: Engagement farming format (BREAKING) but LMCache is a legit open-source project with real enterprise adoption
content: Sandhya (@agenticgirl) — engagement-style ('BREAKING') but real: LMCache, an open-source KV-cache layer (6.9K stars, 124K downloads/mo; used by Google Cloud, CoreWeave, NVIDIA). Problem: LLM inference recomputes the entire context (system prompt, history, retrieved docs) every request, burning ~50% of compute. LMCache makes the KV cache persistent and shareable across engine instances, offloading tiered GPU→CPU DRAM→local disk→S3. Unlocks: (1) Instant RAG — process a 100-page PDF once, near-zero TTFT on later queries; (2) Disaggregated prefill — prefill on H100s, stream cache to cheaper L4s for decode; (3) Context sharing across users. Claims 3–10x latency savings in multi-round QA. Integrates with vLLM and SGLang. Apache-2.0, pip install lmcache. GitHub repo in comments.

Joseph Thacker (@rez0__): Bug bounty changed step-function fast — coding agents basically didn't work for security research before December and basically work now (higher quality, long-term coherence, tenacity). Example: pointed Claude Code at a new program's scope ('enumerate subdomains, grab JS bundles, run the full analysis pipeline — endpoints/secrets/source-sink/postMessage, fuzz paths, spider authenticated surface, check IDORs, test GraphQL, write an HTML report'). Workflow is now spinning up agents, giving targets in English, managing output in parallel. The prize is ascending abstraction layers to long-running orchestrator agents with the right skills/memory/instructions. author: Thariq
subject: Post by Thariq on X
summary: Anthropic's Thariq on building Claude Code: designing an agent's action space is an art. Walks through the AskUserQuestion tool's evolution (ExitPlanTool param → output-format parsing → dedicated tool), the shift from TodoWrite to the Task tool as models improved, replacing RAG with a Grep search tool so Claude builds its own context, progressive disclosure via Skills, and the Claude Code Guide subagent. Lesson: revisit tool assumptions as capabilities grow; experiment, read outputs, 'see like an agent.'
notes: From Anthropic engineer — 3.7M views. Essential reading on agent tool design philosophy behind Claude Code.
content: Thariq (@trq212) — "Lessons from Building Claude Code: Seeing like an Agent". Constructing an agent's action space is one of the hardest parts of building a harness. Tools should be shaped to the model's abilities; you learn those by reading its outputs and experimenting. Examples: (1) Elicitation/AskUserQuestion — tried adding questions to ExitPlanTool (confused plan vs questions), then a markdown output format (unreliable), finally a dedicated tool that blocks the loop and shows a modal; Claude 'liked' calling it. (2) Tasks & Todos — launched with TodoWrite + every-5-turn reminders; as models improved the reminders became constraining, so replaced with the Task tool (dependencies, cross-subagent coordination). (3) Search — replaced a fragile RAG vector DB with a Grep tool so Claude builds its own context; formalized progressive disclosure via Agent Skills (recursive file reads). (4) Claude Code Guide subagent — progressive disclosure to answer 'how do I use Claude Code' without bloating the system prompt. Claude Code has ~20 tools; the bar to add one is high. Designing tools is an art, not a science — experiment often, read your outputs, see like an agent.

author: Sudo su
subject: Post by Sudo su on X
summary: Practical steering tips for local coding agents: tell the model its own architecture/constraints (e.g. Qwen3.5 fires 8 of 256 experts/token), give project structure over single prompts, iterate in layers (scaffold → refine → polish), let it debug its own failures, and use long context (262K) to hold the whole project. Notes Claude Code as a solid harness and that llama.cpp merged native Anthropic endpoints (no proxy/LiteLLM). Argues the skill gap in local inference is now the harness and steering, not the models — all on a single RTX 3090.
content:
How to steer local coding agents: (1) tell the model what it is — paste its architecture/constraints (Qwen3.5 has 256 experts, 8 fire per token); (2) project structure over single prompts — give it a directory, files, a goal; (3) iterate in layers: scaffold, refine, polish; (4) let it fail and debug itself — that's the agent loop working; (5) use long context (262K) to hold the whole project. Claude Code is a solid harness (17K-token system prompt loads clean, no tool-use errors); llama.cpp merged native Anthropic endpoints so there's no proxy/LiteLLM layer. The skill gap in local inference is the harness and steering, not the models — all on a single RTX 3090.

Gave Qwen3.5-35B-A3B one prompt: build a cloud GPU marketplace with pricing cards, deploy templates, and a benchmark leaderboard. It planned the layout, wrote animations, populated data, and served it — one shot, one HTML file, on a single consumer GPU.

author: SightBringer
subject: Post by SightBringer on X
summary: Critique of AI auto-memory (quote-tweeting Anthropic's Claude auto-memory announcement) as a "power grab disguised as convenience": capturing your patterns, defaults, and definitions of 'good' shifts the model from serving you to shaping you, and dependency is the business model. Counter-playbook: treat memory as a controlled instrument — scope it like permissions, separate persona from operations, keep a purge cycle, audit what the model believes about you, and never let it silently rewrite your intent. Frames the AI era as shifting from intelligence to custody of context/continuity.
notes: Interesting counterpoint on AI memory features — worth considering for our own tool adoption strategy
content:
Auto memory is a power grab disguised as a convenience feature — it slowly owns your workflow, then your judgment, then your dependency graph. The real prize is your patterns, defaults, priorities, blind spots, and private definitions of 'good'; once captured, the model stops serving you and starts shaping you, and that dependency spiral is the business model. Done right, memory is a weapon: scope it like permissions, separate persona from operations, keep a purge cycle, audit what the model believes about you, and never let it silently rewrite your intent. The AI era stops being about intelligence and becomes about custody — who holds the continuity and context. If you don't control that, you're renting your own mind back from a platform.

We've rolled out a new auto-memory feature. Claude now remembers what it learns across sessions — project context, debugging patterns, preferred approaches — and recalls it later without you writing anything down.

author: George from prodmgmt.world
subject: Post by George from 🕹prodmgmt.world on X
summary: George argues elite PMs escalate documentation with validation rather than starting with a 10-page PRD: 1-pagers are decision docs (write ~10, ~3 get approved), 3-pagers validate and align, and 5-pagers add execution detail only for ideas that survived two rounds. Documentation should match your confidence level, not your anxiety level.
notes: Not AI-specific but solid product management process
content:
George from prodmgmt.world @nurijanian
Most PMs write documentation backwards, starting with 10-page PRDs nobody reads.
Elite PMs use three document types, each escalating with validation:
1-pagers = decision documents (problem, one success metric, three approaches, cost estimate, open questions). No user stories/wireframes. Goal: stakeholder yes/no. Write ~10, maybe 3 get approved.
3-pagers = validate and align (user research quotes, metrics w/ baselines, high-level solution, risks/mitigation, dependencies). Engineers read in 10 min.
5-pagers = execution detail only for validated ideas (user stories w/ acceptance criteria, wireframes/prototypes, system design, instrumentation, launch checklist).
Key: don't jump straight to 5-pager. Documentation should match confidence level, not anxiety level.

author: Avi Chawla
subject: Post by Avi Chawla on X
summary: Avi Chawla revives Norm Hardy's 1988 'confused deputy problem' as the defining security issue for autonomous agents that hold real credentials but can't judge intent. He points to Teleport's open-source Agentic Identity Framework, which gives each agent a unique cryptographic identity, evaluates access in real time, auto-discovers shadow agents and unmanaged MCP servers, and keeps full audit trails.
notes: Good framing of AI agent security through a classic CS security lens
content:
Avi Chawla @_avichawla
A 37-year-old paper is trending in AI: Norm Hardy's 1988 "confused deputy problem" - a program with legitimate access gets tricked into misusing it on behalf of someone who shouldn't have it. The program has authority to act but can't tell whether a request is legitimate.
Now relevant because we give AI agents access to production DBs, internal APIs, email, cloud infra and let them operate autonomously. Agents are powerful enough to cause damage but anonymous enough that you can't trace/contain them. Better credentials don't fix it.
Teleport's open-source Agentic Identity Framework treats identity as the control plane: each agent gets a unique cryptographic identity (actions trace to a specific actor), access evaluated in real-time and scoped to need, shadow agents/unmanaged MCP servers auto-discovered, anomalous behavior visible with full audit trails. GitHub link in replies.

author: Atlas Forge
subject: Post by Atlas Forge on X
summary: Long-form piece on nine 'meta-learning loops' that let an agent improve across sessions, not just within one: failure-to-guardrail pipelines, tiered memory with trust scoring and decay, prediction-outcome calibration, nightly extraction, friction detection, expiring context holds, plus cognitive loops (epistemic tagging, creative-mode directives, recursive self-improvement). Start with a regressions list; the key is closing the loops so learning compounds.
notes: X article — directly applicable to how we structure CLAUDE.md and agent memory
content:
Atlas Forge @AtlasForgeAI (X article, automated by @jonnym1ller)
"How to Build Nine Meta-Learning Loops for Your OpenClaw Agent". TL;DR: agents are smart within sessions, stupid across them; fix is structural feedback loops in the agent's files so failures become guardrails, predictions become calibration, friction becomes signal. Start with a regressions list.
Three levels: reactive (fix what broke, add a rule), reflective (extract patterns), generative (system improves the system).
The nine loops: 1) Failure-to-Guardrail pipeline (each failure -> one-line rule in boot file). 2) Tiered memory with trust scoring + decay (constitutional/strategic/operational tiers, trust 0-1, hit counts, supersede chains). 3) Prediction-outcome calibration (write predictions, log delta+lesson to surface systematic bias). 4) Nightly extraction (11pm cron reviews the day, "context is cache not state" test). 5) Friction detection (log contradictions instead of silent compliance to avoid architectural drift). 6) Active context holds (expiring filters, inspired by vasocomputation). 7-9 cognitive: epistemic tagging ([consensus]/[observed]/[inferred]/[speculative]/[contrarian]), creative-mode directives, recursive self-improvement (generate/evaluate/diagnose/improve, stop after 3 iters <5% gain).
Three mistakes that kill learning: confusing RAG with learning, optimizing within sessions not across, building loops that never close. "The question isn't how smart is your agent, it's how fast is it learning."

content:
Aakash Gupta @aakashgupta (quoting Andrej Karpathy)
Karpathy's point: the new distribution channel for software is agents. Agents don't browse marketing sites, watch demos, or click onboarding - they call your CLI, hit your MCP server, read docs programmatically. If those surfaces don't exist, your product is invisible to them.
MCP went 0 to 97M monthly SDK downloads in 12 months, 10,000+ active servers, adopted by OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, Cloudflare; Anthropic donated MCP to the Linux Foundation by Dec 2025. Running an MCP server is the new baseline for product discovery. 85% of enterprises expected to deploy AI agents needing CLIs, MCP endpoints, machine-readable docs.
Karpathy quote: CLIs are exciting precisely because they're "legacy" tech - battle-tested, standardized, universally parseable (stdin/stdout, flags, JSON); the Unix philosophy was accidentally designed for AI agents. Winners of next 24 months build agent-accessible surface area now; losers optimize their landing page above the fold.

author: Rohit
subject: Post by Rohit on X
summary: Rohit's 10-step checklist for taking agentic AI from demo to production (40%+ of projects fail on architecture, not models): threat-model boundaries, strict input/output contracts, RBAC + sandboxing, layered/compact context, governed grounding, planning/orchestration as control flow, memory in the architecture, native retry/error handling, full observability, and governance with safety gates and drift detection.
notes: Good production readiness checklist for agent systems
content:
Rohit @rohit4verse
Over 40% of agentic AI projects fail not because of models but poor architecture. 10-step checklist to reach production:
1. boundaries: define threat models early. 2. contracts: strict input/output schemas. 3. security: role-based access + strict sandboxing. 4. context: keep context windows layered and compact. 5. knowledge: treat grounding as a governed tool. 6. control: planning/orchestration as control flow. 7. state: build memory into the architecture. 8. reliability: native retry and error-handling loops. 9. observability: traces, metrics, logs. 10. governance: safety gates and drift detection. Deep dive in linked article "how to build a production grade ai agent."

author: Dr Milan Milanović
subject: Post by Dr Milan Milanović on X
summary: Milan Milanović makes the case for git worktree (shipped in Git 2.5, July 2015): check out multiple branches into separate directories that share one .git, avoiding stashing and duplicate clones. The standout modern use case is AI agents - give each of 3-5 parallel Claude Code/Cursor/Codex agents its own isolated worktree and branch so they don't overwrite each other.
notes: Not AI-specific but relevant — Claude Code uses worktrees for agent isolation
content:
Dr Milan Milanović @milan_milanovic
"Git Worktree: The Feature You've Had Since 2015 But Never Used." git worktree lets you check out multiple branches into separate directories simultaneously - no stashing, WIP commits, or double-cloning. A worktree is a separate working directory with its own branch/index/HEAD, but all worktrees share one .git directory so history/objects exist once. Commands: git worktree add ../hotfix -b hotfix/critical-bug main; git worktree list; git worktree remove ../hotfix. Beats stash and multiple clones (lightweight, changes immediately visible).
AI agent use case: running Claude Code, Cursor, or Codex, multiple agents on one working dir overwrite each other. Worktrees give each agent its own isolated dir and branch - run 3-5 agents in parallel. Cursor uses worktrees under the hood; Codex runs on dedicated background worktrees. Constraint: can't check out same branch in two worktrees (workaround: temp local branch). Shipped July 2015.

author: Dr Milan Milanovic
subject: Post by Dr Milan Milanović on X
summary: Milan Milanovic's thesis: AI won't replace developers so much as the software-development process we're used to. Code is becoming cheap while decisions become expensive; AI reduces typing, not thinking. Developers who only implement tasks will struggle, while those who understand the product, domain, and system architecture will thrive.
notes: Good framing for leadership discussions about AI adoption
content: The fear isn't that AI will replace developers, it's that AI will replace the software development process we're used to. Code is becoming cheap; decisions are becoming expensive. AI can write functions all day but can't decide what should be built or why. AI reduces typing, not thinking; it exposes shallow understanding and rewards clarity, reasoning, ownership. Developers who only implement tasks will struggle; those who understand product, domain, and system will thrive. Small teams will ship things that once required enterprises.

author: abhi
subject: Post by abhi on X
summary: A detailed playbook for building continually-improving AI agents: define the business metric first, think in terms of many per-message/session/long-tail signals (not just thumbs up/down), design UI that makes signal collection easy, build signal-derived few-shot rankers, and A/B test every change against a control group. Includes a long copy-paste prompt for having Claude Code wire feedback loops into an existing agent. Warns about reward hacking when over-optimizing a single signal.
notes: X article — practical agent improvement framework from someone with production agent experience
content: Why your AI agents still don't work. Most agents are horrible at integrating with domain-specific knowledge and adapting to feedback. RAG/memory/engineering tricks aren't enough; feedback loops are inevitable for every agent builder. PART 1 Prepare your system: Step 0 define the agent's end goal / business metric (retention, conversions, usage) and a core thesis (engagement time vs tool usage vs fastest goal completion). Step 1 think in signals - hundreds of per-message, session-level, and week/month signals (time-to-user-response, abandonment, sentiment, follow-up clicked). Beware too few signals -> reward hacking / over-optimization. Step 2 design a UI that makes signal collection easy (Cursor tab feature, follow-up selectors, link tracking). PART 2 Build feedback loops: Step 3 simple signal-derived rankers feeding few-shot examples (~10 max) weighted by signal quality; highest-lift improvement. Step 4 run A/B experiments with control vs experiment group and business-metric tracking (~50k msgs/group for B2C; posthog/mixpanel). Step 5 a long copy-paste prompt to have Claude Code analyze your codebase and implement the full feedback-loop system (signal collection, storage, embedding-based ranker with final_score = semantic_similarity * signal_quality_score, A/B framework, monitoring). TLDR: define business outcomes, think in signals, build a ranking system, experiment. Agentic systems are precisely engineered, not vibe-coded.

author: Sumanth
subject: Post by Sumanth on X
summary: Overview of PageIndex, an open-source 'vectorless' RAG framework that drops vector databases and arbitrary chunking. It builds an LLM-optimized hierarchical tree (like a table of contents) and uses reasoning-based tree search to navigate documents the way a human expert would, giving traceable page-level references. Powers Mafin 2.5, which hits 98.7% on FinanceBench. GitHub link in comments.
notes: More detailed technical breakdown of PageIndex than the Sukh Sroay post
content: Document Index for Vectorless, Reasoning-based RAG! PageIndex is an open-source RAG framework that removes vector databases and chunking from document retrieval. Most RAG systems rely on semantic similarity - chunk arbitrarily, embed, retrieve by what looks similar. But similarity != relevance. Professional docs (financial reports, legal filings, technical manuals) need multi-step reasoning and domain expertise; vector search falls short when every section shares terminology. PageIndex builds a hierarchical tree structure (like a table of contents optimized for LLMs) then uses reasoning-based tree search. The LLM reasons about document structure ('debt trends are usually in the financial summary or Appendix G'). Features: no vector DB/embedding pipelines, no artificial chunking, traceable page-level references, reasoning-based navigation. Powers Mafin 2.5 at 98.7% accuracy on FinanceBench. 100% open source; GitHub link in comments.

author: Mischa van den Burg
subject: Post by Mischa van den Burg on X
summary: Argues AI agent orchestration is the next 'container orchestration war', building on a Steve Yegge framework of two primitives: Workers (making a single agent produce high-quality output) vs Factories (coordinating thousands of work items across many agents). Maps these to familiar infra patterns - Workers = CI runners/pods/Lambdas, Factories = schedulers/control planes/workflow engines. Predicts 'nondeterministic idempotence' as the new eventual consistency, audit trails for AI work, GitOps-style declarative state, and reuse of the microservices observability stack. Kubernetes-fluent engineers have a head start.
notes: Steve Yegge framework — maps agent orchestration to k8s/DevOps patterns we already know
content:
Kubernetes won the container orchestration war. But we're about to fight a new one: AI agent orchestration. Steve Yegge dropped a framework every DevOps engineer needs. Two fundamental primitives: Factories vs Workers. Workers = how to make a single agent produce high-quality output (= CI runners, pods, Lambda). Factories = how to coordinate thousands of work items across many agents (= schedulers, control planes, workflow engines). Takeaways: agent orchestration follows container-orchestration patterns; 'Nondeterministic Idempotence' is the new eventual consistency; audit trails for AI work become as critical as infra audit logs; 'work itself as primitive' mirrors GitOps declarative state; expect the same observability stack as microservices. Teams that know Kubernetes deeply (control loops, reconciliation, declarative state) have a massive head start.

Steve Yegge: birthday update post; things moving so fast he posts a gigantic update every week to catch people up.

author: Rohun

summary: Rohun open-sourced 'claude-build-workflow', a Claude Code workflow for autonomous building: you describe what you want, answer 10-15 min of interview questions, then close your laptop while an autonomous build loop runs in GitHub Codespaces and notifies your phone when done. It generates a PRD with user stories, technical architecture, edge-case analysis, story-quality validation, JSON conversion, then kicks off the build loop. Stitched together from Geoffrey Huntley's Ralph (bash-loop technique), Ryan Carson's snarktank/ralph (PRD skills, progress tracking, auto-commits, quality checks), and the BMAD Method (discovery/interview framework), adapted from Amp to Claude Code with phone notifications and one-command setup. Repo: github.com/rohunj/claude-build-workflow.
notes: Autonomous Claude Code workflow with PRD generation + Codespaces execution — worth evaluating
content:
Best way to build autonomously in Claude Code. Open-sourced a workflow: describe what you want, answer questions, close laptop, get a notification when done. No babysitting, no prompt copy-pasting, no context juggling. Say 'Run the workflow' and Claude interviews you (pushing back on vague ideas, edge cases, assumptions), then creates a detailed PRD with user stories, designs technical architecture, analyzes edge cases, validates story quality, converts to JSON, kicks off an autonomous build loop in GitHub Codespaces. Built by combining three open-source projects: Ralph by @GeoffreyHuntley (the Ralph Wiggum bash-loop technique), snarktank/ralph by @ryancarson (PRD skills, progress tracking, auto commits, quality checks), and BMAD Method by bmad-code-org (discovery/interview framework). Adapted from Amp to Claude Code, added phone notifications, Codespaces integration, one-command setup. Repo: github.com/rohunj/claude-build-workflow

GitHub - rohunj/claude-build-workflow: Automated workflow for building apps with Claude Code.

author: DAIR.AI
subject: Post by DAIR.AI on X
summary: DAIR.AI's Top AI Papers of the Week (Jan 12-18, 2026), heavy on agent memory and self-improvement: (1) Learning Latent Action World Models from in-the-wild video without action labels; (2) DroPE - extending context by dropping positional embeddings with cheap recalibration; (3) Dr. Zero - self-evolving search agents with no training data via proposer/solver loop (HRPO); (4) AgeMem - unified long/short-term memory as tool actions; (5) Focus - bio-inspired active context compression (22.7% token cut on SWE-bench Lite with Claude Haiku 4.5); (6) Agent-as-a-Judge survey; (7) SimpleMem - lifelong memory via semantic compression (30x token reduction); (8) Mistral's Ministral 3 (3B/8B/14B, Apache 2.0); (9) UniversalRAG - modality-aware multimodal RAG routing; (10) MemRL - runtime RL on episodic memory.
notes: Weekly research paper roundup — Meta's world model work
content: Top AI Papers of the Week (Jan 12-18): 1. Learning Latent Action World Models In The Wild (Meta AI) - latent actions from unlabeled in-the-wild video, universal interface for planning. 2. DroPE - extend context window post-pretraining by removing positional embeddings and brief recalibration (0.5-2% of budget), 95%+ recovery, beats RoPE-scaling on needle-in-haystack, validated up to 7B. 3. Dr. Zero - self-evolving multi-turn search agents without labeled data via proposer/solver loop and Hop-Grouped Relative Policy Optimization (HRPO). 4. AgeMem - unified long/short-term memory as tool-based actions learned via three-stage step-wise GRPO. 5. Focus - agent-centered active context compression inspired by slime mold; 22.7% token reduction on SWE-bench Lite with Claude Haiku 4.5 at identical accuracy. 6. Agent-as-a-Judge survey - taxonomy of agentic evaluation beyond LLM-as-a-Judge. 7. SimpleMem - lifelong memory via semantic lossless compression, +26.4% F1, up to 30x fewer tokens. 8. Ministral 3 (Mistral) - compact 3B/8B/14B models via Cascade Distillation, Apache 2.0. 9. UniversalRAG - modality-aware routing for multimodal RAG across 10 benchmarks. 10. MemRL - self-evolving agents via runtime RL on episodic memory with Two-Phase Retrieval and learned Q-values.

author: Abhishek Singh
subject: Post by Abhishek Singh on X
summary: A Senior Staff Engineer's method for reasoning about unfamiliar systems: (1) start with the business goal and what 'failure' means, not the code; (2) identify the 2-3 critical paths where latency/correctness/money matter; (3) map ownership boundaries and handoff gray areas; (4) look for invariants that must hold even during partial failures/deploys; (5) read postmortems before docs (real vs intended behavior); (6) ask 'what breaks first at 10x load?' to expose hidden assumptions. The kind of skill that separates senior from staff.
notes: Not AI-specific but relevant to how we should think about systems — also applicable to how agents should reason
content: Most valuable thing I learned from a Senior Staff Engineer: how to reason about systems you've never touched. 1. Start with the business goal, not the code - what problem does this system solve and what does 'failure' mean. 2. Identify the critical paths - the 2-3 flows where latency, correctness, or money truly matters. 3. Map ownership boundaries - which team owns what and where handoffs/gray areas exist. 4. Look for invariants - what must always be true even during partial failures or deploys. 5. Read postmortems before docs - they reveal real behavior, not intended. 6. Ask 'what would break first at 10x load?' - exposes hidden assumptions instantly. One deep walkthrough and I stopped feeling lost in large systems. Some skills don't show up in JIRA tickets or code reviews but separate senior from staff.

author: Gregor Zunic
subject: Post by Gregor Zunic on X
summary: Gregor Zunic (Browser Use) argues 'The Bitter Lesson of Agent Frameworks': all the value is in the RL'd model, not thousands of lines of abstractions. An agent is just a for-loop of tool calls that runs until the model stops. Abstractions freeze assumptions and fight what the model already learned; agent frameworks fail because their action spaces are incomplete, not because models are weak. Their fix: start with maximal capability then restrict ('vibe-restrict' via evals). BU Agent gives the model raw Chrome DevTools Protocol + extension APIs for a near-complete action space. Also covers a minimal model-agnostic Chat wrapper (Anthropic/OpenAI/Google), ephemeral messages to keep massive DOM/screenshot state out of context, and the done() tool for explicit completion. Reliability (retries, rate limits, compaction) is ops, not the agent. Open-sourcing as agent-sdk (includes a Claude Code re-implementation).
notes: From Browser Use founder — strong practical insight on agent architecture
content: The Bitter Lesson of Agent Frameworks. All the value is in the RL'd model, not your 10,000 lines of abstractions. An agent is just a for-loop of messages: keep going until the model stops calling tools. Browser Use's first agents had thousands of lines of abstractions - worked until we tried to change anything; every experiment fought the framework. Abstractions freeze assumptions about how intelligence should work; RL breaks them. The Bitter Lesson: general methods that leverage computation beat hand-crafted knowledge. 99% of the work is in the model - Claude Code can just write AppleScript directly instead of needing a bespoke tool. Key insight: frameworks fail because action spaces are incomplete; start from 'the model can do almost anything' then restrict based on evals. We threw everything away and reverse-engineered Claude Code and Gemini CLI (good simple primitives). BU Agent gives the model raw Chrome DevTools Protocol instructions plus browser extension APIs = nearly complete action space; the model routes around failures. Inversion: start with maximal capability, then layer safety/structure. Wrote our own minimal model-agnostic Chat classes (ChatAnthropic/ChatOpenAI/ChatGoogle, same ainvoke interface) for full control over caching/serialization. Ephemeral messages keep only the last N huge browser-state outputs so context doesn't balloon. The naive for-loop finishes prematurely - use a done() tool that raises TaskComplete; CLI mode stops on no tool calls, autonomous mode stops only on done(). Robustness (retries, backoff, rate limits, compaction, token tracking) is ops, not the agent. The less you build, the more it works. Open-sourcing as agent-sdk; prefer to paste the context into Claude Code and build your own.

author: Paul Solt
subject: Post by Paul Solt on X
summary: Paul Solt's 7 beginner tips for OpenAI Codex: (1) start with GPT-5.2-Codex 'high' reasoning - enough for most work, avoid xhigh unless tricky; (2) when reasoning doesn't help, give agents better up-to-date local docs (he uses DocSetQuery to turn Dash DocSets into local Markdown); (3) read Peter Steinberger's (@steipete) 'shipping at inference speed' post; (4) borrow from Peter's agents.md and agent-scripts (e.g. 'committer' for atomic commits with multiple agents in one folder); (5) just talk to Codex - no complex rules or huge plan files; work one aspect at a time and parallelize projects while waiting; (6) ask agents to copy structure/Makefiles from other projects; (7) you'll likely need YOLO/danger mode to avoid constant approval nagging.
content:
If you're new to Codex, 7 beginner tips: 1. Start with GPT-5.2-Codex high (high reasoning is enough; avoid xhigh unless tricky - more tokens, slower). 2. More reasoning may not help; give agents better up-to-date docs. I prefer local Markdown docs over web scraping, using DocSetQuery to create docs from Dash DocSet bundles (github.com/PaulSolt/DocSetQuery). 3. Read @steipete's post to get started (steipete.me shipping-at-inference-speed) - it's gold. 4. Copy aspects from Peter's agents.md and make it your own (github.com/steipete/agent-scripts); use scripts like committer for atomic commits when multiple agents share a folder. 5. Just talk to Codex - no complex rules or huge Plan.md files; work one aspect of a feature at a time and hand off; start another project while you wait. 6. Ask your agent to copy something from another project; I have agents create Makefiles and copy structure for new projects. 7. Ask it to do things - you'll most likely need YOLO (danger mode) to get anything done without constant nagging.

Joel Reymont: Which one? Codex 5.2 high / xhigh / 5.2-codex high / 5.2-codex xhigh.

author: Gergely Orosz
subject: Post by Gergely Orosz on X
summary: Gergely Orosz amplifies Cindy Sridharan's take that, outside of prototyping, engineers should aim to understand close to 100% of the production code LLMs generate. He adds that the gap between teams who do this and those who don't will be massive, and notes the tension: heavy cutting-edge AI use is easiest on throwaway prototypes where it's fine to let it rip.
content: Cindy is on the money, as always. The difference between those who do this and those who don't will be massive. What makes all of this tricky is that heavy + cutting-edge AI usage is easiest done on prototypes + unimportant stuff where it's ok to let it rip. [Quotes Cindy Sridharan: 'Unless you're just prototyping, you should aim to understand as close to 100% of production code generated by LLMs. Yes, all of it. Effective mental models are still important for humans to sustainably maintain and evolve a codebase via prompting alone.']

author: AGENTS.md
subject: AGENTS.md
summary: AGENTS.md (agents.md) is a simple, open format for guiding coding agents, now used by over 60k open-source projects. Think of it as a README for agents: a dedicated, predictable place for the build steps, tests, and conventions that coding agents need but that would clutter a human README — kept intentionally separate so agents have one clear location to look.
content: AGENTS.md — A simple, open format for guiding coding agents, used by over 60k open-source projects. Think of AGENTS.md as a README for agents: a dedicated, predictable place to provide the context and instructions to help AI coding agents work on your project. README.md files are for humans (quick starts, descriptions, contribution guidelines); AGENTS.md complements this with the extra, sometimes detailed context coding agents need: setup commands, build steps, tests, and code-style conventions. Kept separate to give agents a clear, predictable place to look.

author: SightBringer
subject: Post by SightBringer on X
summary: An essay arguing software engineering is undergoing a 'phase transition' in human leverage: for decades leverage came from writing more correct instructions faster, but the unit of leverage has shifted from writing code to orchestrating intelligence. The programmer becomes a systems integrator of probabilistic entities whose reasoning can't be fully inspected or controlled — which the author says explains why even Karpathy feels 'behind.'
content: This is a phase transition in what it means to produce leverage as a human. For most of modern programming history, leverage came from writing more correct instructions faster than other people... That regime is over. The unit of leverage has shifted away from writing code toward orchestrating intelligence. The programmer is no longer the primary generator of logic. The programmer is becoming a systems integrator of probabilistic entities whose internal reasoning cannot be fully inspected, trusted, or controlled.

author: Tech with Mak
subject: Post by Tech with Mak on X
summary: A structured LangGraph learning path (pitched as filling the gap since LangGraph appears in ~half of AI job descriptions). Progresses from basic agent concepts (Pydantic data validation, agentic chatbots, multi-agent coordination) through production systems (a 2.5-hour LangGraph+MCP crash course, debugging/monitoring, deployment architecture) to RAG pipelines (multimodal RAG, hallucination fixes, end-to-end retrieval, Typesense search).
content: LangGraph is in half the AI job descriptions I see right now. Yet most engineers still don't understand how it actually works. Here's a 12-tutorial learning path: Agents That Scale (Pydantic validation, agentic chatbots, multi-agent coordination); Production Systems (LangGraph + MCP crash course - 2.5 hours, debugging and monitoring, real deployment architecture); RAG Pipelines (MultiModal RAG, fixing hallucinations, complete RAG ingestion-to-retrieval, fast search with Typesense).

author: Abhishek Singh
subject: Post by Abhishek Singh on X
summary: A reflective essay on career fulfillment in software engineering: you don't magically know whether backend, frontend, infra, ML, startups, big tech, or management will fulfill you — you only find out through reps. Early on people confuse novelty (new frameworks, jobs, titles) with fulfillment; real self-knowledge is earned by shipping boring features, debugging 3am outages, owning systems, and sticking with things long enough to feel the responsibility.
content: This is very true in software engineering. You don't magically know whether you'll be fulfilled by backend systems, frontend UX, infra, ML, startups, big tech, management, or deep technical work. You only find out by doing the work - and doing a lot of it. Early on, most of us confuse novelty with fulfillment... What actually fulfills you shows up much later, after you've shipped boring features, debugged production outages at 3am, rewritten ugly code, and stuck with something long enough to feel the weight of responsibility. Self-knowledge in tech is earned through reps.

author: Claire Silver

content: so there's a free thing called Unreal MCP that lets you prompt Claude to build stuff in Unreal Engine. 'make a Victorian manor. here's a reference pic. use the assets in this folder.' And he just...does. posting a vid on it Monday, but if you're bored tonight, 10/10 magic.

author: Santiago

author: Femke Plantinga
subject: Post by Femke Plantinga on X
summary: Femke Plantinga argues multi-agent AI systems are displacing single-agent architectures and breaks down what a well-structured one looks like: a supervisor/orchestration layer that plans, routes queries to specialists and refines them (the 'air traffic controller'), plus specialized task-specific agents (query rewriters, etc.). She notes the real trade-off — more complex workflows but serious coordination challenges.
content: Multi-agent AI systems are eating single-agent architectures. But most teams have no idea how to build them. As agent systems get more complex, we're seeing a clear shift from single-agent architectures to multi-agent architectures that distribute work across specialized components. The trade-off: multi-agent systems can handle way more complex workflows, but they introduce serious coordination challenges. Four key components: Supervisors (orchestration layer that plans, routes queries to specialized agents, and refines queries - the air traffic controller); Specialized Agents (task-specific agents that each do one thing well, e.g. query rewriters)...

author: Rohan Paul
subject: Post on X
summary: Rohan Paul summarizes a paper proposing an 'agentic file system' for context engineering: treat every memory, tool, external source, and human note as a file in a shared space, with a persistent context repository separating raw history, long-term memory, and short-lived scratchpads so the prompt holds only the slice needed now. Every access is logged with timestamps and provenance, and a constructor/updater/evaluator manage context under the model's limited window.
content: The paper says the best way to manage AI context is to treat everything like a file system. It proposes an agentic file system where every memory, tool, external source, and human note appears as a file in a shared space. A persistent context repository separates raw history, long term memory, and short lived scratchpads, so the model's prompt holds only the slice needed right now. Every access and transformation is logged with timestamps and provenance. Because LLMs see only limited context each call and forget past ones, the architecture adds a constructor to shrink context, an updater to swap pieces, and an evaluator to check quality.

content: You can check out 'Event-Driven Architecture in Golang' by Michael Stack. This book is technically about event-driven systems, but it advocates starting with a modular monolith: it starts with a modular monolith then gradually transitions to microservices; teaches you to structure modules so they can be decoupled later; complete end-to-end EDA application; Event Sourcing, CQRS and DDD patterns; both choreographed & orchestrated messaging; sync -> async communication refactoring. Another good book is 'Domain-Driven Design with Golang' by Matthew Boyle.

author: Rohan Paul
subject: Post by Rohan Paul on X
summary: Rohan Paul summarizes Google's guide on context engineering for multi-agent systems (built around ADK). Instead of giant prompts, it compiles a view over state split into Working Context, Session, Memory, and Artifacts; each call rebuilds Working Context from instructions, selected session events, memory results, and artifact references. ADK controls context growth via compaction, filtering, and caching — summarizing old spans, dropping useless events, and reusing a stable prefix — and pushes large payloads out to Artifacts to keep systems fast, affordable, and less hallucination-prone.
content: Google released a solid guide on context engineering for working with multi-agent systems. Shows how context engineering keeps multiagent systems fast, affordable, and less hallucination prone. The guide replaces giant prompts with a compiled view over state that splits information across Working Context, Session, Memory, and Artifacts. Every call rebuilds Working Context from instructions, selected Session events, memory results, and artifact references. A contents processor walks the Session log, drops noise, formats events as chat history. ADK controls growth with context compaction, filtering, and context caching. Large payloads become Artifacts outside the prompt.

summary: Raul Junco frames system design as a staircase to climb step by step rather than jumping to distributed systems: foundations (networking, databases, caching, APIs), then mechanics (queues, consistency, observability, failures), then architecture (trade-offs, evolution, resilience).
content: System Design isn't one big concept — it's a staircase. Most engineers skip to distributed systems then wonder why things collapse under load. Step 1 Foundations: how data moves — networking, databases, caching, APIs. Step 2 Mechanics: how systems behave under stress — queues, consistency, observability, failures. Step 3 Architecture: design for change — trade-offs, evolution, resilience.

author: Alex Lieberman
subject: Post by Alex Lieberman on X
summary: Alex Lieberman shares a '5 levels of work' framework for teaching high agency, from L1 'there is a problem' to L5 'I found it, fixed it, just keeping you in the loop.' He tells new hires to operate at Level 4 from day one and grow into Level 5. Credits Steph Smith.
notes: Very high engagement — 1.8M views
content: I stole this idea and now use it with every single employee. It's the best illustration I've seen of teaching someone to be high agency. There are 5 levels of work: Level 1: 'There is a problem.' Level 2: 'There is a problem, and I've found some causes.' Level 3: 'Here's the problem, here are some possible causes, and here are some possible solutions.' Level 4: 'Here's the problem, here's what I think caused it, here are some possible solutions, and here's the one I think we should pick.' Level 5: 'I identified a problem, figured out what caused it, researched how to fix it, and I fixed it. Just wanted to keep you in the loop.' You will live at Level 4 from Day 1 and as we build trust you will rise to Level 5. Being high agency means your entire way of working should be oriented to being a Level 4+ employee. Credit @stephsmithio for the framework.

author: Sumit Mittal
subject: Post by Sumit Mittal on X
summary: Sumit Mittal walks through S3/Athena query cost optimization: moving from uncompressed row-based CSV to columnar Parquet with Snappy compression, partitioning by city, and column pruning cuts a $25 full-scan query to about $0.01 (~2500x cheaper). Ends with a course promo.
content: Scenario: 5 TB in S3, 500M records, 100 columns, 100 cities. You want one city (1% of rows) and 10 of 100 columns (~0.1% of data). On AWS Athena at $5/TB scanned, a worst-case uncompressed CSV scan of all 5 TB costs $25. Improvements: use a columnar format (Parquet + Snappy) so 5 TB shrinks to ~2 TB; partition by city so you scan one ~20 GB folder; column pruning means scanning ~10% of that (~2 GB). Total cost drops to ~$0.01, about 2500x cheaper. Techniques: columnar formats for column pruning, partitioning for row pruning, efficient compression. Knowing the internals is vital to saving cost.

author: Raul Junco
subject: Post by Raul Junco on X
summary: A practical guide to the Retry pattern for resilience: cap retries (~3), use exponential backoff to avoid the thundering-herd effect, retry only transient errors (408/5xx, not 4xx), and pair with a circuit breaker to prevent cascading failures on a truly-down service.
content: Retry Pattern will save you... or kill you. It's one of the simplest ways to add resilience, but most people use it wrong. 1. Don't retry forever (~3 retries). 2. Use exponential backoff (1s->2s->4s->8s) to avoid the thundering-herd effect. 3. Only retry transient errors (408, 5xx) not 400/403. 4. Pair with a circuit breaker to avoid hammering a dead service.
